milestone_description: Transform RASS into a fully async, ETL-provenance-aware ingestion platform with a proper document registry and the foundation for Bring-Your-Own Knowledge Base (BYO KB). These are the structural changes needed before adding higher-order retrieval or enterprise features.

issues.title: [Phase B] Implement async document ingestion pipeline with job queue
issues.body:
## Problem

The current `embedding-service` processes document uploads synchronously inside the HTTP request handler. For large files (PDFs with hundreds of pages, DOCX, etc.) this causes:
- HTTP timeouts on the client side for large documents
- Inability to report progress to the user
- No retry mechanism for failed ingestion jobs
- Blocking the Node.js event loop during CPU-intensive parsing and chunking
- No visibility into the queue depth or throughput

This is a critical reliability and scalability gap for an enterprise-grade system.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Introduce BullMQ (backed by Redis) for job queue management
- Add `bullmq` dependency to `embedding-service/package.json`
- Create `embedding-service/src/queue/ingestionQueue.js` — defines the named queue `rass:ingestion`
- Create `embedding-service/src/workers/ingestionWorker.js` — processes jobs: parse → chunk → embed → index
- Move all ingestion logic from the HTTP route handler into the worker

### 2. Refactor the upload HTTP endpoint
- The `/upload` route should validate the file, write it to a temp location, enqueue a job, and immediately return a `{ jobId, status: 'queued' }` JSON response (202 Accepted)
- Use `multer` with `diskStorage` so the file path can be passed into the job payload

### 3. Add a job-status polling endpoint
- `GET /ingest/status/:jobId` — returns `{ jobId, status, progress, error?, completedAt? }`
- BullMQ's `Job.fromId()` provides all required fields

### 4. Frontend integration
- Update the upload component to poll `/ingest/status/:jobId` every 2 s after upload
- Show a progress bar / spinner with status text (Queued → Processing → Complete / Failed)

### 5. Failure handling and retries
- Configure BullMQ with `attempts: 3, backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 5000 }`
- On permanent failure, write error details to the document registry (see separate issue)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Uploading a document returns HTTP 202 with a `jobId` within 500 ms regardless of file size
- [ ] A worker processes the ingestion job asynchronously and emits progress events (0 → 25 → 75 → 100 %)
- [ ] `GET /ingest/status/:jobId` returns correct status at each stage
- [ ] Failed jobs are retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff before being marked `failed`
- [ ] The frontend displays a live progress indicator during ingestion
- [ ] Existing unit/integration tests pass; new tests cover the queue and worker
- [ ] BullMQ dashboard (Bull Board) is accessible at `/admin/queues` in the development environment

issues.title: [Phase B] Add ETL provenance tracking and audit logging
issues.body:
## Problem

Today RASS has zero visibility into *how* a document was processed. There is no record of:
- Which chunking strategy was applied (parameters, overlap, method)
- Which embedding model version produced each vector
- How long each ETL stage took
- Whether the content was modified (e.g., OCR applied, encoding normalized)
- Who triggered the ingestion

Without provenance, debugging retrieval quality issues is guesswork, and re-indexing decisions have no data basis.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Design a provenance schema
Add a `DocumentProvenance` Prisma model in `mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma`:
```
model DocumentProvenance {
  id               String   @id @default(cuid())
  documentId       String   // FK to DocumentRegistry
  userId           String
  ingestionJobId   String
  rawFileSha256    String   // hash of the original file bytes
  fileType         String
  fileSizeBytes    Int
  pageCount        Int?
  chunkingStrategy Json     // { method, chunkSize, overlap }
  embeddingModel   String   // e.g. "text-embedding-3-large"
  embeddingDim     Int
  chunkCount       Int
  parentCount      Int
  stagesMs         Json     // { parse, chunk, embed, index } durations
  createdAt        DateTime @default(now())
}
```

### 2. Instrument the ingestion worker
- Wrap each stage (parse, chunk, embed, index) with `performance.now()` timing
- Compute SHA-256 of the raw file bytes before parsing
- After successful indexing, persist a `DocumentProvenance` row via the mcp-server's internal API or direct DB write

### 3. Expose provenance via the API
- `GET /api/documents/:documentId/provenance` — returns the provenance record for a document
- Include provenance summary in the document list response

### 4. Audit log for user actions
- Log all user-triggered events (upload, delete, search) to a structured audit table or append-only log file with ISO timestamps, userId, action, and outcome

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Every successfully ingested document has a `DocumentProvenance` record in Postgres
- [ ] `stagesMs` accurately records parse/chunk/embed/index wall-clock times
- [ ] `rawFileSha256` is computed and stored; duplicate uploads of the same file are detectable
- [ ] `GET /api/documents/:documentId/provenance` returns all provenance fields
- [ ] Audit events (upload, delete, query) are written to the audit log with userId and timestamp
- [ ] Prisma migration for the new model is included and runs on `migrate deploy`

issues.title: [Phase B] Build document registry with metadata, status, and lifecycle management
issues.body:
## Problem

Currently, RASS has no centralised record of which documents have been ingested. Documents live as chunks in OpenSearch and parent blobs in Redis, but there is no:
- Single source of truth for document identity
- Status tracking (uploading, processing, ready, failed, deleted)
- Metadata store (filename, size, MIME type, upload timestamp, owner)
- Way to list, filter, or paginate a user's documents
- Mechanism to delete a document and all its associated chunks/parents

This makes the system opaque to users and prevents building features like workspace management, quota enforcement, and document-level permissions.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add `Document` model to Prisma schema
```
model Document {
  id               String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId           String
  originalFilename String
  mimeType         String
  fileSizeBytes    Int
  status           DocumentStatus @default(QUEUED)
  chunkCount       Int?
  errorMessage     String?
  openSearchIndex  String   @default("knowledge_base")
  redisKeyPrefix   String?
  uploadedAt       DateTime @default(now())
  processedAt      DateTime?
  deletedAt        DateTime?
  provenance       DocumentProvenance?
  User             User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}

enum DocumentStatus {
  QUEUED
  PROCESSING
  READY
  FAILED
  DELETED
}
```

### 2. Wire the registry into the ingestion pipeline
- On upload: create a `Document` row with status `QUEUED`, return its `id` as the `documentId`
- On worker start: update status to `PROCESSING`
- On success: update status to `READY`, set `chunkCount`, `processedAt`
- On failure: update status to `FAILED`, set `errorMessage`

### 3. Document management API endpoints
- `GET /api/documents` — paginated list of current user's documents (filter by status)
- `GET /api/documents/:id` — single document metadata
- `DELETE /api/documents/:id` — delete document: remove OpenSearch chunks, Redis parents, update status to `DELETED`

### 4. Frontend document library
- Add a "My Documents" panel listing all ingested documents with status badges
- Add a delete button with confirmation
- Show file size, upload date, chunk count

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Every upload creates a `Document` registry entry and the `documentId` is returned to the client
- [ ] Document status transitions correctly: QUEUED → PROCESSING → READY (or FAILED)
- [ ] `GET /api/documents` returns a paginated, filterable list scoped to the authenticated user
- [ ] `DELETE /api/documents/:id` removes all associated OpenSearch vectors and Redis parents, then marks the DB record DELETED
- [ ] Frontend shows a live document library with status badges
- [ ] Orphaned chunks (from failed jobs) are cleaned up on retry or after configurable TTL

issues.title: [Phase B] Implement configurable chunking strategies
issues.body:
## Problem

The current chunking logic in `embedding-service/index.js` is hardcoded: fixed parent chunk size (2000 chars), fixed child chunk size (200 chars), fixed overlap. There is no way to:
- Adjust chunking parameters per document type or user preference
- Experiment with different strategies (semantic, sliding window, sentence-aware)
- Compare retrieval quality across chunking configurations
- Support documents that need different strategies (e.g., legal contracts vs. research papers)

Hardcoded chunking is a known limiting factor for retrieval quality.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Extract chunking into a strategy module
Create `embedding-service/src/chunking/` with:
- `ChunkingStrategy.js` — abstract base class / interface
- `FixedSizeChunker.js` — current behavior (configurable `chunkSize`, `overlap`)
- `RecursiveCharacterChunker.js` — LangChain `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` wrapper
- `SentenceWindowChunker.js` — splits on sentence boundaries, creates sliding windows
- `index.js` — factory function `createChunker(strategy, options)`

### 2. Read strategy from config
Add to `config.yml`:
```yaml
CHUNKING_STRATEGY: recursive_character  # fixed_size | recursive_character | sentence_window
PARENT_CHUNK_SIZE: 2000
PARENT_CHUNK_OVERLAP: 200
CHILD_CHUNK_SIZE: 400
CHILD_CHUNK_OVERLAP: 50
```

### 3. Store strategy in provenance
- Record the chosen strategy name and parameters in `DocumentProvenance.chunkingStrategy`

### 4. Per-upload override (optional)
- Allow an optional `chunkingStrategy` field in the upload multipart form to override the default

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The chunking strategy is read from `config.yml` and applied during ingestion
- [ ] At least three strategies are implemented and selectable via config
- [ ] Changing `config.yml` and re-ingesting a document produces different chunk counts
- [ ] The selected strategy and parameters are stored in `DocumentProvenance.chunkingStrategy`
- [ ] A unit test for each strategy verifies expected chunk boundaries and counts

issues.title: [Phase B] Lay the foundation for Bring-Your-Own Knowledge Base (BYO KB)
issues.body:
## Problem

Currently, all documents are ingested into a single shared OpenSearch index (`knowledge_base`) and are distinguished only by `userId` metadata filtering. This prevents:
- Creating multiple isolated knowledge bases for different projects or domains
- Sharing a KB across multiple users/teams
- Assigning different retrieval parameters (embedding model, search weights) per KB
- Building the workspace model required for Phase D enterprise readiness

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Introduce the `KnowledgeBase` concept in the data model
Add to Prisma schema:
```
model KnowledgeBase {
  id              String     @id @default(cuid())
  name            String
  description     String?
  ownerId         String
  isPublic        Boolean    @default(false)
  openSearchIndex String     @unique  // e.g. "kb_<cuid>"
  embeddingModel  String     // inherit from config or override
  embedDim        Int
  createdAt       DateTime   @default(now())
  documents       Document[]
  members         KBMember[]
  Owner           User       @relation(fields: [ownerId], references: [id])
}

model KBMember {
  id              String   @id @default(cuid())
  kbId            String
  userId          String
  role            KBRole   @default(VIEWER)
  KB              KnowledgeBase @relation(fields: [kbId], references: [id])
  User            User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}

enum KBRole { OWNER EDITOR VIEWER }
```

### 2. Provision a dedicated OpenSearch index per KB
- On `KnowledgeBase` creation, create an OpenSearch index named `kb_<id>` with the same mapping as the current default
- Embed dimension must match `embeddingModel`

### 3. Update ingestion to target the KB's index
- Upload request includes a `kbId` parameter
- Embedding worker indexes into the KB's dedicated OpenSearch index
- Retrieval passes the KB's index name to OpenSearch queries

### 4. API endpoints for KB management
- `POST /api/knowledge-bases` — create KB
- `GET /api/knowledge-bases` — list accessible KBs
- `DELETE /api/knowledge-bases/:id` — delete KB and all its indices

### 5. Default KB migration
- Each existing user gets a default KB provisioned automatically; their existing documents are logically associated with it

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A user can create multiple named KBs; each gets its own OpenSearch index
- [ ] Documents can be ingested into a specific KB by passing `kbId` in the upload request
- [ ] Retrieval is scoped to a specific KB index
- [ ] KB membership (`KBMember`) controls access: only members with `VIEWER` role or above can query
- [ ] Deleting a KB deletes its OpenSearch index, Redis parents, and all associated documents
- [ ] The Prisma migration is included and passes `migrate deploy` without errors
- [ ] Existing users are automatically provisioned a default KB on first login post-migration
